repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
Problem
When receiving `Content-Length` messages, the RPC client allocates a new string just to use `find` for searching.

## Solution
Instead of allocating an extra string, perform a byte-by-byte search via `buf:ref()`. By supplementing our string buffer shim, we ensure it still runs on PUC Lua.

comment_data.body:
```suggestion
      -- Find the header boundary "\r\n\r\n" (compare bytes instead of string.find(), to avoid a string alloc).
```
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -191,22 +191,29 @@ local default_dispatchers = {
 }
 
 --- @async
-local function request_parser_loop()
-  local buf = strbuffer.new()
+local function parse_content_length()
+  local strbuf = strbuffer.new()
   while true do
-    local msg = buf:tostring()
-    local header_end = msg:find('\r\n\r\n', 1, true)
-    if header_end then
-      local header = buf:get(header_end + 1)
-      buf:skip(2) -- skip past header boundary
+    local header_len ---@type integer?
+    local ptr, len = strbuf:ref()
+    for i = 0, len - 4 do
+      -- Find the header boundary "\r\n\r\n".

comment_data.body: This was a missing feature from our shim 👍
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -99,8 +103,27 @@ function StrBuffer:reset()
   return self
 end
 
+--- Efficiently read the character at the 0-based index `k` in the buffer.
+--- @param k integer
+--- @return integer
+function StrBuffer:_index(k)
+  return self:_normalize().buf[1]:byte(k + 1)
+end
+
+--- @return vim._core.stringbuffer.ptr, integer
+function StrBuffer:ref()

comment_data.body: this is a microoptimization, but it's only ~4 lines and easy to revert.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -191,22 +191,30 @@ local default_dispatchers = {
 }
 
 --- @async
-local function request_parser_loop()
-  local buf = strbuffer.new()
+local function parse_content_length()
+  local strbuf = strbuffer.new()
   while true do
-    local msg = buf:tostring()
-    local header_end = msg:find('\r\n\r\n', 1, true)
-    if header_end then
-      local header = buf:get(header_end + 1)
-      buf:skip(2) -- skip past header boundary
+    local header_len ---@type integer?
+    local ptr, len = strbuf:ref()
+    for i = 0, len - 4 do
+      -- Find the header boundary "\r\n\r\n"
+      -- (compare bytes instead of string.find(), to avoid a string alloc).
+      if ptr[i] == 13 and ptr[i + 1] == 10 and ptr[i + 2] == 13 and ptr[i + 3] == 10 then

comment_data.body: > The rename to parse_content_length seems misleading. The function is the msg decoding coroutine yielding the json messages. It internally uses get_content_length to figure out how much bytes it needs to decode the body, but that's not the main aspect for a user of the function. With parse_xy functions I'd expect the function to return a parsed xy. That's not the case here, the content_length isn't returned/exposed

Could we have not done something like?

```suggestion
      if ptr[i] == '\r' and ptr[i + 1] == '\n' and ptr[i + 2] == '\r' and ptr[i + 3] == '\n' then
```
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -191,22 +191,30 @@ local default_dispatchers = {
 }
 
 --- @async
-local function request_parser_loop()
-  local buf = strbuffer.new()
+local function parse_content_length()
+  local strbuf = strbuffer.new()
   while true do
-    local msg = buf:tostring()
-    local header_end = msg:find('\r\n\r\n', 1, true)
-    if header_end then
-      local header = buf:get(header_end + 1)
-      buf:skip(2) -- skip past header boundary
+    local header_len ---@type integer?
+    local ptr, len = strbuf:ref()
+    for i = 0, len - 4 do
+      -- Find the header boundary "\r\n\r\n"
+      -- (compare bytes instead of string.find(), to avoid a string alloc).
+      if ptr[i] == 13 and ptr[i + 1] == 10 and ptr[i + 2] == 13 and ptr[i + 3] == 10 then

comment_data.body: That makes sense; the renaming here is because this PR is cherry-picked from #39000, where I am no longer using coroutines, so it might be accurate there but not here. We can see how #39000 turns out. If we ultimately decide to continue using coroutine, we can give it a new name.

The result of `ptr[i]` is a `number` (or say, `integer`). Lua does not have a char type, only strings; the suggested approach would attempt to compare a `number` with a `string`, which will always result in `false`. In the code before this PR, `get_content_length` used many such comparisons, such as `c >= 65 and c <= 90`, for this same reason.

The only viable approach would be `('\r'):byte(1)`, but it doesn't seem worth it.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -191,22 +191,30 @@ local default_dispatchers = {
 }
 
 --- @async
-local function request_parser_loop()
-  local buf = strbuffer.new()
+local function parse_content_length()
+  local strbuf = strbuffer.new()
   while true do
-    local msg = buf:tostring()
-    local header_end = msg:find('\r\n\r\n', 1, true)
-    if header_end then
-      local header = buf:get(header_end + 1)
-      buf:skip(2) -- skip past header boundary
+    local header_len ---@type integer?
+    local ptr, len = strbuf:ref()
+    for i = 0, len - 4 do
+      -- Find the header boundary "\r\n\r\n"
+      -- (compare bytes instead of string.find(), to avoid a string alloc).
+      if ptr[i] == 13 and ptr[i + 1] == 10 and ptr[i + 2] == 13 and ptr[i + 3] == 10 then

comment_regular_data.body: There is still one string allocation remaining: `strbuf:get(header_len)`. However, `get` is well-suited for the consume pattern used here. This can be further improved in #39000. @lewis6991 @mfussenegger I’d appreciate your thoughts on this.

Here is the benchmark script: https://gist.github.com/ofseed/5c373e4641f0a3013175a4d19f9074c0. `tostring+find` is the previous method, while `ref+scan` is the method used in this PR. Here are the results: ``` buffer_size=1024 bytes iterations=1000 marker_pos=64 tostring+find time=0.001874s avg=1.874us/op checksum=64000 ref+scan time=0.000346s avg=0.346us/op checksum=64000 ``` - `buffer_size` represents the size of the buffer being processed (which significantly impacts `tostring+find` due to new string allocations) - `marker_pos` represents the position of the content being searched for within the buffer (which significantly impacts `ref+scan` because the search is performed in Lua rather than C). Here is how these two variables change: Actually, I didn't intend to draw this graph, but I fell into a trap in the earliest version of the script: Lua does not allocate a new string when the string content is identical. This made `tostring+find` appear much faster, whereas `ref+scan` slowed down more significantly as `marker_pos` increased. However, that was incorrect; the current benchmark is more representative of reality. Summary: - The `find` in `tostring+find` is faster than `scan` because it is implemented in C, but it allocates a new string; - `ref+scan` performs the scan in Lua, which is slower, but it does not allocate a new string; However, the time saved by reducing string allocations is significantly more substantial than the time saved during the search, so `ref+scan` is faster.

title: perf(lsp): avoid unnecessary string allocations when parsing content-length
